1: shema Yisrael (šĕmaʿ yiśrāʾēl): 'Hear, O Israel' — the Shema-formula opens every major address in Deuteronomy; hearing (obedient attention) is the covenant stance. La'avotar goyim (lĕhaḵrît gôyim): 'to dispossess nations greater and mightier' — gadol (greater) and atzum (mightier) are exactly the terms used at Kadesh-barnea to describe the paralyzing impossibility; now they introduce a possibility in YHWH's strength.
2: The Anakites (anakim) — the giant-descendants of Anak; the spies' report had made them the paradigm of military impossibility (Num 13:33: 'we were like grasshoppers in our own eyes'). The proverb 'mi yityatzev lifnei benei Anak?' ('who can stand before the sons of Anak?') preserves the cultural terror these warriors generated. Deuteronomy answers the rhetorical question: YHWH can, and Israel will, because YHWH fights for them.
3: esh okhlah (ʾēš ʾōkelāh): 'a consuming fire' — the first occurrence of this theophanic description in Deuteronomy 9; it recurs in 4:24 and Heb 12:29. The fire-character of YHWH is his holiness in active engagement with what resists him. Ha-over lefanecha ('going before you') — YHWH as the advance combat force; Israel follows the divine warrior's trail. The promise is triple: destroy (yashmid), subdue (yakniyam), and drive out (vehorashtam).
4: al-tomar bilevavcha betzidqati (ʾal-tōmar bilibabĕkā bĕṣidqāṯî): 'do not say in your heart because of my righteousness' — the conquest of Canaan tempts Israel to interpret divine favor as moral achievement. The correction: the nations' dispossession is berishtat haggoyim ('because of the wickedness of these nations'), not Israel's merit. This verse is the anthropological ground for Paul's argument in Romans: before God, no one can say 'because of my righteousness.'
5: lo betzidqatcha uveyosher levavcha (lōʾ bĕṣidqāṯĕkā ûbĕyōšer lĕbābĕkā): 'not because of your righteousness or the uprightness of your heart' — the double denial eliminates both behavioral righteousness (tzaddiq) and motivational integrity (yashar) as grounds for the gift. The positive ground is twofold: (1) berishtat haggoyim (the nations' wickedness); (2) lema'an hakim et-hadavar (to fulfill the covenant oath to the patriarchs). Grace, not merit, is always the covenantal operative.
6: am qesheh oref (ʿam qĕšēh-ʿōrep): 'stiff-necked people' — the defining characterization that Moses levels at Israel; the ox that will not yield its neck to the yoke. This designation first appears at Exod 32:9 after the golden calf; Deuteronomy 9 returns to it as the theological counter to any self-congratulatory interpretation of the conquest. Stephen's use of this exact phrase in Acts 7:51 shows its enduring force as the diagnosis of Israel's pattern of resistance.
7: zakhor al-tishkach (zĕkor ʾal-tiškaḥ): 'remember, do not forget' — the two imperatives set up the antithetical pairing that structures Deuteronomic ethics. Mamrim heyitem ('you have been rebellious'): marah means to rebel, to be bitter, to be defiant — the full weight of covenant-legal disobedience. Meyom da'ati etchem ('from the day I knew you') — Moses's pastoral knowledge as witness; the entire history is a cumulative record.
8: Chorev (ḥōrēb): Deuteronomy's name for Sinai; the same site where YHWH revealed himself in fire and gave the law. Le'hashmidechem ('to destroy you'): the same root (shamad) used for the nations Israel will displace. The parallel is deliberate: Israel's faithlessness makes them liable to the same destruction as the nations. The difference is intercessory grace (v18-19, 25-29).
9: luchot avanim luchot ha-berit (luḥôṯ ʾăbānîm luḥôṯ habbĕrîṯ): 'tablets of stone, the covenant tablets' — the apposition identifies the stone tablets as the material form of the covenant; to receive the tablets is to receive the covenant. Lechem lo akhalty umayim lo shatiti ('bread I did not eat and water I did not drink') — the complete fast matches the duration; Moses sustained on divine presence alone, a proleptic sign of the eschatological feast where YHWH is the provision.
10: ketuvim be-etzbah Elohim (kĕṯûbîm bĕʾeṣbaʿ ʾĕlōhîm): 'written by the finger of God' — the identical phrase as Exod 31:18; the Decalogue's divine authorship is emphasized precisely where it would become most important: Israel has just demonstrated why they need a law they cannot write themselves. The etzbah (finger) of God that writes the law is the same etzbah that drove out Egypt's plagues (Exod 8:19) and that Jesus invokes in Luke 11:20.
11: The forty days concluded: vayyitten YHWH elai et-shney luchot ha-avanim luchot ha-berit ('the LORD gave me the two stone tablets, the covenant tablets'). The handing over (natan) is formal and deliberate — the covenant document transferred from divine hand to human mediator. Verse 11 is the structural hinge: the tablets given just as the people below have abandoned them.
12: maher saru min-haderekh (māher sārû min-haddārek): 'they have quickly turned from the way I commanded them' — maher (quickly, soon) is the word of shocking speed: Moses has not even descended with the covenant tablets when Israel constructs its replacement. Pesel masekah ('cast metal idol') — the melted earrings shaped into the calf constitute the precise inversion of the covenant tablets: human-made versus God-written, metal image versus invisible word.
13: am qesheh oref (ʿam qĕšēh-ʿōrep): 'a stiff-necked people' — repeated from v6 for emphasis; YHWH's own diagnosis of Israel's character. The repeated assessment both explains the apostasy (they are constitutionally resistant) and grounds the need for grace (there is no remedy from within Israel's own nature). This is the OT anthropology that Paul assumes in Romans 7.
14: herepha mimmenni veashmidem (herepeʾ mimmennî wĕʾašmîdēm): 'leave me alone that I may destroy them' — YHWH presents himself as restrained by Moses's presence; the intercessory posture of the mediator as the space between divine wrath and its object. Emcheh et-shemam ('I will blot out their name'): name-erasure is covenant death — to have no name in Israel is to have no future; compare Rev 3:5 (not blot out from the book of life). The Moses-replacement offer (a mightier nation from Moses) anticipates the new creation community.
15: Moses descends veha-har bor be-esh ('while the mountain was burning with fire'), the two covenant tablets in his hands — the physical tension between the covenant-document in his grip and the covenant-broken scene below. The fire-mountain is the covenant's witness; Moses descends from YHWH's presence into the catastrophe that presence indicts.
16: chet'em laYHWH Eloheichem (ḥăṭāʾtem laYhwh ʾĕlōhêkem): 'you had sinned against the LORD your God' — the covenant-legal vocabulary; chata is missing the mark, failing the covenant standard. Egel masekah ('a cast calf') — the bull-image of El/YHWH syncretism; not the worship of another god but the worship of YHWH under a prohibited form. The sin is representational before it is substitutional.
17: Moses's tablet-breaking: va'etpos bishnei haluchot va'ashlikh otam me'al shetei yadai ('I seized the two tablets and threw them from both my hands') — the enacted covenant-dissolution; shattering the treaty document was the ancient Near Eastern symbolic act for covenant termination. Moses breaks the tablets before the people's eyes as a prophetic sign-act: you have already broken what these tablets contain. The breaking is not anger management but theology in action.
18: The second prostration — forty days and forty nights; the threefold parallel with v9 and v25 structures the intercession narrative. Kol-chatatechem asher chatatum la'asot hara be'einei YHWH ('all your sin that you committed by doing what was evil in the sight of the LORD') — the comprehensive summary; ra be'einei YHWH ('evil in the eyes of YHWH') is Deuteronomy's standard formula for covenant breach.
19: chamat YHWH ve-apo (ḥămaṯ Yhwh wĕʾappô): 'the fury of the LORD and his anger' — the pairing of chemah (burning wrath) and af (anger/nostril-flaring) intensifies the danger; YHWH's wrath is not mild discomfort but covenant-breach consequence. Vayyishma YHWH elai gam bappa'am hazeh ('the LORD listened to me that time as well') — the effectiveness of intercession; prayer changes the divine-human trajectory. Moses's intercessory effectiveness is the OT type of Christ's high-priestly prayer (Heb 7:25).
20: be'Aharon hitanaf meod l'hashmido (bĕʾahărōn hiṯʾannaṯ mĕʾōd lĕhašmîdô): 'very angry with Aaron to destroy him' — Aaron's priestly leadership of the calf-construction makes him secondarily liable for the covenant disaster. Moses intercedes specifically for Aaron (va'etpallel gam be'ad Aharon ba'et hahiv, 'I prayed also for Aaron at that same time'). The high priest is saved by intercessory prayer; the greater High Priest (Heb 5:1) is himself the intercessor who cannot be destroyed.
21: The destruction of the calf follows a precise protocol: burned (saraph ba'esh), crushed (khatash), ground fine (tachon heitev), scattered in the stream. The thoroughness mirrors the herem-destruction of Canaanite idols commanded in Deut 7:5, 25. Total destruction is the only adequate response to the idol; partial preservation leaves the possibility of return. The dust thrown into the stream recalls Exod 32:20 — the consuming of the idol by its former worshippers.
22: The catalog of wilderness failures: Taberah (tab'erah, 'burning' — the fire incident of Num 11:1-3), Massah (massah, 'testing' — the water-from-rock complaint of Exod 17:1-7), Kibroth-hattaavah (qibrot hatta'avah, 'graves of craving' — the quail-excess of Num 11:31-35). Each place-name is a theological memory: the wilderness geography is sin-geography, the landscape of Israel's pattern of provoking YHWH.
23: lo-he'emantem lo velo shmahtem beqolo (lōʾ heʾĕmantem lô wĕlōʾ šmaʿtem bĕqōlô): 'you did not trust him and did not obey his voice' — the two verbs of covenant failure at Kadesh: unbelief (aman hiphil, to trust/believe) and disobedience (shama to listen/obey). The Kadesh failure is not primarily military but theological: the spies' report was believed over YHWH's promise. Hebrews 3:16-19 uses this as the definitive example of the unbelief-disobedience connection.
24: mamrim heyitem im-YHWH meyom da'ati etchem (mamrim hĕyîtem ʿim-Yhwh miyyôm daʿtî ʾeṯkem): 'you have been rebellious against the LORD from the day I first knew you' — the sweeping pastoral indictment; Moses's entire relationship with Israel has been a relationship with rebels. Da'ati ('from my knowing you') frames it relationally: Moses knows Israel not as a supervisor knows employees but as a shepherd knows sheep. The rebellion is all the more grievous for its relational context.
25: The second extended intercession: forty days and forty nights, prostrate before YHWH (va'etnapal lifnei YHWH et arba'im hayom ve'et arba'im halaylah asher hitnapalti). The three prostration periods (vv9, 18, 25) structure chapter 9's narrative of grace through intercession. Moses's physical posture — face-to-the-ground — enacts the absolute vulnerability of the mediator before the covenant-keeping God on behalf of the covenant-breaking people.
26: Adonai YHWH al-tashchet ammecha venachalatecha (ʾăḏōnāy Yhwh ʾal-tašḥēṯ ʿammĕkā wĕnaḥălāṯekā): 'O Lord GOD, do not destroy your people and your inheritance' — the double divine name (Adonai YHWH) of solemn address; the same formula Moses used at Deut 3:24. The intercessory argument is possessive: they are your people, your inheritance. YHWH's own honor is at stake in their preservation. Asher padita begadolecha ('whom you redeemed by your greatness'): the exodus-redemption as the basis for the appeal.
27: zechor la'avadeicha le-Avraham le-Yitzchak ule-Ya'aqov (zĕkor lĕʿăbādêkā lĕʾabrāhām lĕyiṣḥāq ûlĕyaʿăqōb): 'remember your servants Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob' — the covenant-history argument; the patriarchal promise creates a divine obligation that cannot be abandoned without self-contradiction. Al-tefen el-qeshiyut ha'am hazeh ('do not consider the stubbornness of this people') — Moses asks YHWH to look past the present character evidence to the prior covenant commitment.
28: pen yomru ha'aretz asher hotzeitanu mishsam (pen-yōmrû hāʾāreṣ ʾăšer hôṣēʾṯānû miššām): 'lest the land from which you brought us say...' — the nations-as-audience argument; the watching world will interpret Israel's destruction as evidence of YHWH's inability (mibli yecholet YHWH, 'because YHWH was not able') or hatred (umissin'ato otam). Divine reputation in the watching world is grounds for covenant preservation. This argument recurs in Moses's intercession at Num 14:13-16 and in the exile-passages of Ezek 36:20-23.
29: vehem ammecha venachalatecha (wĕhēm ʿammĕkā wĕnaḥălāṯekā): 'but they are your people and your inheritance' — the closing possessive assertion; the intercessory prayer ends with the same possessive argument it began with (v26). The prayer's logic: they belong to you, you redeemed them, your reputation requires their survival, your covenant to the patriarchs demands their continuation. The bare possessives — your people, your inheritance — are the ultimate appeal: ownership creates obligation.
